evidence.title: MLOps Governance, Evolution, and Architectural Patterns on AWS
extracted_claims: The transition to mature MLOps on AWS by 2026 is driven by the need to scale complexity while embedding robust governance.

Critical challenges in MLOps governance include managing multi-account complexity, ensuring end-to-end data lineage, achieving model explainability (XAI), detecting bias/fairness, and maintaining security and compliance at scale.
Overall Platform Governance can be established using AWS Control Tower and Service Catalog to create a secure, multi-account landing zone with standardized deployment templates.
Data Lineage and Auditability are supported by AWS CloudTrail, AWS Config, and ML Governance.
Model Explainability is addressed by AWS SageMaker Clarify and Prescriptive Guidance.
Bias Detection is achieved through SageMaker Clarify and Custom Monitoring integrated into the ML workflow.
The focus for MLOps evolution in AWS by 2026 is deep, unified integration centered around Amazon SageMaker.
The Feature Store is evolving to be a data-centric component, integrating technologies like Apache Iceberg and Streaming for real-time feature serving.
The architecture trend is towards deeper integration between the data layer (Lake Formation/Glue), the modeling layer (SageMaker Pipelines), and the feature layer (SageMaker Feature Store).
A hybrid architecture is dominant, combining serverless compute (Lambda), container orchestration (ECS/EKS), and workflow orchestration (Step Functions) for scalability.
Container orchestration (ECS/EKS) is preferred for heavy-compute ML workloads (e.g., large training jobs), while Serverless Functions (Lambda) are ideal for event-driven, low-latency inference.
AWS Step Functions is central for orchestrating complex, multi-stage MLOps workflows, providing state management, error handling, and reliability.
Amazon SageMaker is leveraged for managed training, hosting, and monitoring, reducing operational burden compared to managing raw infrastructure.

research_gaps: Specific performance benchmarks or ROI data related to implementing these governance frameworks by 2026 are missing.
Detailed examples or migration strategies for integrating Apache Iceberg and Streaming into the AWS Feature Store are not provided.
Specific regulatory requirements (e.g., specific mandates for XAI or bias detection) that drive the governance frameworks are not detailed.
Cost implications associated with the hybrid architecture (ECS/EKS vs. Lambda) in a large-scale MLOps context are not addressed.
